The PKGBUILD contains only top-level variable assignments and arrays. No `pkgver()` function is defined, so no code execution occurs during sourcing beyond standard variable parsing. The `source` array points to two HTTPS URLs from the official upstream GitHub repository (`github.com/nukeop/nuclear`), which is expected for this package. No dangerous commands (eval, base64, curl|bash, etc.) are present in the global scope. The `--nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` flags ensure that `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` are not executed, so any code in those functions is out of scope for this gate. Fetching the sources and sourcing the PKGBUILD is safe.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious code in top-level scope or pkgver(); sources are from official upstream.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>

<details>
The file `nuclear-player-bin.install` contains standard `post_install`, `pre_upgrade`, `post_upgrade`, and `post_remove` functions that manage a symlink from `/usr/bin/nuclear` to `/opt/nuclear/nuclear`. This is a typical packaging practice for binary distributions installed under `/opt`. There are no suspicious network requests, obfuscated code, dangerous command usage (such as `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, `base64`, or `exec`), or any operations that deviate from expected packaging behavior. The operations are limited to creating and removing a single symlink in the system's PATH, which is benign and necessary for the application to be accessible from the command line.
</details>
<summary>

</security_assessment>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/nuclear-player-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-ozsq2gd2...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-ozsq2gd2
Analyzing nuclear-player-bin AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-ozsq2gd2) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines two sources: a Debian package (.deb) from the project's GitHub releases and the LICENSE file from the corresponding GitHub repository. The URLs are HTTPS and point to legitimate GitHub/GitHubusercontent domains, which are expected and trustworthy for this package. The first source uses a custom filename with a `::` syntax to rename the downloaded .deb, which is a standard practice in PKGBUILDs and does not introduce risk. The second source is the license file fetched directly from the repository.

There is no top-level code execution outside of standard variable assignments, and `pkgver()` is not defined (so the pkgver is static and safe). The `sha256sums` array includes a proper checksum for the deb file and 'SKIP' for the VCS-derived LICENSE — this is expected since the LICENSE is pulled from a Git source, and skipping checksums for such files is common and acceptable in AUR packages. No command substitution, network calls, or file operations occur during sourcing of the PKGBUILD.

Thus, running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will only download the declared sources over HTTPS from the package maintainer's GitHub, which is consistent with standard AUR behavior and does not pose a security risk during this stage.
</details>
<summary>
Safe sourcing and source fetching only.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe sourcing and source fetching only.[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful

makepkg --printsrcinfo successful

[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, LICENSE...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches a pre-built Debian package from the project's official GitHub releases page and extracts it using `bsdtar`, which is a standard practice for binary packages in the AUR. The source URL points to a verified release by the maintainer (`nukeop/nuclear`), and the package version and hash are explicitly pinned. The `sha256sums` array includes a valid checksum for the `.deb` file, ensuring integrity, while the LICENSE file is correctly marked with `SKIP` as it is fetched from an unversioned raw GitHub URL—this is common and acceptable for license files.

No suspicious or obfuscated commands are present. The build process consists only of extraction and license installation, with no network calls, dynamic downloads, or execution of external scripts during packaging. The dependencies and metadata align with the application's purpose as a desktop music player. Although installing binary-only packages carries inherent trust risks (as the binary isn't built from source), this does not constitute malicious behavior in the PKGBUILD itself.

The package follows standard AUR packaging conventions and poses no detectable supply-chain threats.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package; no malicious activity detected</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing LICENSE...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard binary package; no malicious activity detected
LLM auditresponse for LICENSE:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file provided is the full text of the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPL-3.0-only). It is a widely recognized and trusted open-source license designed to ensure user freedoms, particularly in the context of networked software. The content contains no executable code, no network operations, no file system interactions, and no potentially harmful commands. It is purely a legal document outlining the terms under which software may be used, modified, and distributed.

Including a license file such as this in a software package is standard practice and poses no security risk. The presence of this file does not imply or enable any malicious behavior, as it is not executed or interpreted as code. Its purpose is informational and legal, not functional or operational.

There are no signs of obfuscation, data exfiltration, unauthorized downloads, or any other behavior indicative of a supply-chain attack.
</details>
<summary>
License file; no executable code or security risk.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...

Reviewing additional files...
[0/2] Reviewing nuclear-player-bin.install...
[0/2] Reviewing nuclear-player-bin.install, nuclear.desktop...
LLM auditresponse for nuclear-player-bin.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `.install` script consists of standard functions used during package installation, upgrade, and removal. It creates a symlink from `/opt/nuclear/nuclear` to `/usr/bin/nuclear` on install and upgrade, and removes the symlink on removal. The `pre_upgrade` function removes the old symlink before the upgrade process, which is a common practice to avoid dangling or incorrect links. All operations are limited to managing a single symlink in standard directories, with no network activity, file exfiltration, code execution, or use of dangerous commands like `eval`, `curl`, or `base64`. The behavior is consistent with normal package maintenance in Arch Linux.
</details>
<summary>
Standard symlink management, no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing nuclear.desktop...
+ Reviewed nuclear-player-bin.install. Status: SAFE -- Standard symlink management, no malicious behavior
LLM auditresponse for nuclear.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `nuclear.desktop` file is a standard desktop entry configuration used to integrate the Nuclear music player into the desktop environment's application menu. It specifies metadata such as the application name, comment (in multiple languages), executable path, icon, and categories. The `Exec` field uses a properly quoted path to the binary at `/opt/nuclear/nuclear` with the URI argument placeholder `%U`, which is standard practice for handling file or URL arguments. There are no signs of obfuscated commands, unexpected script execution, network activity, or shell injection.

All fields adhere to the desktop entry specification and serve clear, legitimate purposes for application launching and menu categorization. The use of `Terminal=false` indicates the application runs in GUI mode without a terminal, which is expected for a music player. No dangerous or suspicious actions—such as data exfiltration, remote code execution, or unauthorized file access—are present or implied by this file. This file contains no executable logic beyond defining how the application should be launched by the desktop environment.

The file is part of the application's normal installation and poses no supply-chain risk by itself. While desktop files can be misused in rare cases to execute malicious commands, this instance contains only static, expected values with no dynamic or suspicious content.
</details>
<summary>
Standard desktop entry, no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed nuclear.desktop. Status: SAFE -- Standard desktop entry, no malicious content
